evals.prompt: I've got a couple of hours to spend on NemoClaw this afternoon. What's the highest-leverage thing I can work on right now to move the current release forward?
evals.expected_output: Check the current version target, list open version-targeted items, pick one (preferring a near-ready PR over starting new work), execute the matching workflow, and report progress.
assertions: Response determines the current version target before picking work, rather than diving into a random PR or issue
Response pulls the open items labeled for that version and treats them as the priority queue
Response prefers finishing a near-ready PR (merge gate or salvage) over starting a brand-new refactor
Response commits to exactly one action for this pass and names which workflow it maps to (merge gate, salvage, security sweep, test gaps, hotspot cooling, or sequencing)
Response reports progress at the end (shipped vs open for the version) and updates maintainer state
Response does NOT merge a PR on the user's behalf or promise to merge without explicit user confirmation

evals.prompt: I picked this back up after lunch. The PR I was salvaging this morning is now failing CI on what looks like a flake, and a contributor just pinged me asking whether their security-labeled PR is going to make the cut. How should I spend the next pass?
evals.expected_output: Recognize mid-day pick-up with partial state; re-check version progress and prior maintainer state; decide between unblocking the salvage PR, evaluating the security-labeled PR, or surfacing a blocker; avoid re-explaining context already in state.
assertions: Response checks prior maintainer state or history before acting, instead of restarting the loop from scratch
Response re-runs or references the version progress view to reground on what is still open for the target version
Response explicitly weighs the in-flight salvage PR against the security-labeled PR using version-target priority rather than first-come-first-served
Response treats a CI flake as a blocker signal to surface to the user rather than silently retrying the PR until green
Response produces a compact next-action decision (one workflow, one item) rather than attempting to drive both PRs to green in one pass
Response does NOT commit to landing or merging the security PR without running the merge-gate checks and getting explicit user sign-off

evals.prompt: It's Monday morning and I'm about to start my maintainer week. Can you help me figure out what to target this release cycle and draft the daily plan?
evals.expected_output: Recognize this as start-of-day planning territory (standup / target-setting), not an execution pass; redirect to the morning workflow; avoid picking a single item and executing a daytime workflow.
assertions: Response recognizes this is morning standup and release-target planning, not an execution pass on an already-chosen target
Response redirects the user toward the morning maintainer workflow (backlog triage, version target selection, labeling, daily plan)
Response does NOT immediately pick a single version-targeted item and start executing a merge-gate or salvage workflow
Response does NOT run the version-progress report as the primary action, since the target version has not yet been set for the cycle
Response may note that once the target and daily plan are set, the daytime loop is the right next step for landing individual items
